The smallest girl in the village, Elara, possessed a voice so high and clear it could pierce the thickest fog. One day, tired of the darkness, she climbed the tallest hill and began to sing. Her song was not of sorrow, but of space—of soaring birds and boundless light. As her melody rose, it pushed against the heavy slate sky.

The sky, enchanted by the sound, began to lift. Inch by inch, Elara’s song acted like a pillar of invisible crystal. Seeing this, the other villagers joined in, their deep baritones and soft altos adding strength to the pillar. With a mighty crack, the slate shattered into a billion pieces, flying upward to become the stars. The darkness retreated, revealing the blue expanse behind it.

The sky remained high above, held aloft only by the memory of that song. It is said that when the wind howls through the canyons, it is merely the earth humming, reminding the sky to keep its distance.
